name: Nominal vs Structural Typing

laws: Nominal: compatibility requires the same declared name/identity
Structural: compatibility requires a matching shape (members/signatures)
A language may mix them (e.g. structural for tuples, nominal for classes)
x.encoding: C/C++/C#/Java classes & Rust types are nominal; structural appears in TypeScript, Go interfaces, OCaml objects, and C++ duck-typed templates / concepts (shape-checked).

def: Two answers to "when are two types compatible?": nominal typing requires the same declared name; structural typing requires the same shape.
problem: Decide whether type compatibility is based on explicit declared identity or on matching members/signatures.
mechanism: Nominal systems treat two identically-shaped types as distinct unless related by name/declaration (preventing accidental interchangeability). Structural systems accept any value whose shape satisfies the expected members, enabling looser coupling.
benefits: Nominal: prevents accidental mix-ups; clear intent
Structural: flexible, less boilerplate, easy adaptation
Mixing gets benefits of both in one language
limits: Structural: accidental compatibility; weaker intent signalling
Tooling and error messages differ in clarity
failures: Assuming structural compatibility in a nominal language
Two structurally-equal types being wrongly interchanged
whenNot: n/a — every typed language picks a point on this axis
myths: "Same fields means same type" — only under structural typing
